content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 24, 1989


Larry T. Combs
County Administrative Officer
County of Sutter
463 Second Street
Yuba City, CA 95991

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-89-177

Dear Mr. Combs:

This is in response to your request for advice regarding your
responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

Your letter raises questions under other provisions of law,
such as Government Code Section 1090. We do not provide advice
concerning laws other than the Act. Please consult with your
county counsel or the Attorney General's office regarding other
applicable laws which may have an impact on your decision to
purchase a building for the county.

QUESTIONS

1. Under the Act, may you participate in discussions, either
at the staff or board level, regarding whether it is more cost
effective, or in the county's long-term best interest, to purchase
a building, remodel existing space, or erect a new building? The
county is considering, among other options, purchasing a building
owned by a developer who is a client of your wife's business.

2. If you may not participate in discussions regarding the
purchase of a building because of your financial interest in that
decision, may you participate in decisions related to remodeling
existing space or the erection of a new building?

3. If you may not participate in the above discussions, may
you participate in discussions after a decision has been made to

___________________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Larry T. Combs
April 24, 1989
Page 2


either purchase, or not to purchase, the building with respect to
which you have a conflict of interest?

     4. May you participate in occupancy and layout questions
regarding the building that would present a conflict of interest?

     5. May you participate in decisions regarding financing,
construction of a building on county property, timing of future
construction, acquisition of space and related issues?

     6. May you participate in the decision to sell county-owned
land or buildings to finance the county's space needs?

CONCLUSIONS

     1. You may not participate in discussions, either at the
staff or board level, regarding whether it is more cost effective,
or in the county's long-term best interest to purchase a building,
remodel existing space, or erect a new building while the county
is considering purchase of the developer's building as one of its
options. These decisions would foreseeably and materially affect
the developer who is a source of income to you.

     2. You may not participate in discussions regarding the
remodeling of existing space or the erection of a new county
building while the county is considering purchase of the
developer's building as one of its options.

     3. You may not participate in discussions leading to a
determination of which building to purchase, if any. Moreover,
once the decision to purchase a particular building has been
reached, you may not participate in discussions affecting the
purchase of the building if the building selected for purchase is
the one owned by the developer who is a source of income to you.

     4. You may not participate in occupancy and layout questions
regarding the building that would present a conflict of interest
while the county is considering purchase of the developer's
building as one of its options.

     5. You may not participate in decisions regarding financing,
construction of a building on county property, timing of future
construction, acquisition of space and related issues while the
county is considering purchase of the developer's building as one
of its options.

     6. You may not participate in the decision to sell county-
owned land or buildings to finance the county's space needs until
after such time as the county has reached a decision to purchase
or not to purchase the developer's building.

Larry T. Combs
April 24, 1989
Page 3


FACTS

You are the county administrator for the County of Sutter.
The county's present space needs require the relocation of the
county health department. To accommodate the health department,
the county is considering three options: (1) Purchasing an
existing building; (2) Remodeling the building currently in use;
or (3) Erecting a new building.

Your spouse owns and manages a public relations firm and has
performed public relations work for a developer who owns one of
the buildings the county has considered for purchase. None of the
public relations work performed by your spouse for the developer
relates to the building in question. The developer has been a
source of income to your spouse within the past 12 months in
amounts exceeding $500.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making or
participating in a governmental decision in which he knows or has
reason to know he has a financial interest. As the county's
administrative officer, you are a public official. (Section
82048.)

An official has a financial interest in a decision within the
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the official or a
member of his or her immediate family or on:

(c) Any source of income other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institu-
tion in the regular course of business on terms
available to the public without regard to official
status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250) or more in value provided to, received by or
promised to the public official within 12 months
prior to the time when the decision is made.

Section 87103(c).

The owner of one of the buildings under consideration for
purchase by the county has been a source of "income" to your
spouse and thus, for you the owner is a potentially disqualifying
financial interest under Section 87103(c). For purposes of the
Act, your "income" is defined in Section 82030(a) to include "any
community property interest in the income of a spouse."

Your spouse is the owner of a public relations firm. The
firm is a source of income to her, and so are her clients. (Sec-
tion 82030(a).) Your community property interest in her income is

Larry T. Combs
April 24, 1989
Page 4


50 percent of her gross income.  Thus, any client of your spouse
who has paid her $500 or more in gross income during the preceding
12 months is a source of $250 or more in income to you for
purposes of Section 87103(c).

     Your spouse has derived in excess of $500 in gross income
within the past 12 months from her transactions with the developer
who owns one of the buildings at issue.  Therefore, the developer
is a source of $250 or more in income to you in the past year.
(Vassey Advice Letter, No. A-86-201, copy enclosed.)  Because the
developer is a source of income to you, you must abstain from
participating in any decision which will have a foreseeable mate-
rial financial effect on the developer.  (Section 87103(c).)

Participation in Governmental Decisions

     For purposes of our analysis, we must now determine what
constitutes participation in governmental decisions.  A public
official "participates in the making of a governmental decision"
when, acting within the authority of his or her position, he or
she:

          (1)  Negotiates, without significant substan-
tive review, with a governmental entity or private
person regarding the decision; or

          (2)  Advises or makes recommendations to the
decision-maker, either directly or without
significant intervening substantive review, by:

               (A)  Conducting research or making any
investigation which requires the exercise of
judgment on the part of the official or
designated employee and the purpose of which
is to influence the decision; or

               (B)  Preparing or presenting any report,
analysis or opinion, orally or in writing,
which requires the exercise of judgment on the
part of the official or designated employee
and the purpose of which is to influence the
decision.

                    Regulation 18700(c), copy enclosed.

     Participating in discussions related to the purchase of a
building would constitute participation in a governmental deci-
sion.  The conflict-of-interest provisions discussed above ef-
fectively prevent you from participating in any discussions,
conducting research, presenting reports, analysis or opinions, or
exercising your judgment for the purpose of influencing a decision
related to the county's space needs if those activities would
foreseeably and materially affect the developer who is a source of

Larry T. Combs
April 24, 1989
Page 5


income to you in a manner different from the effect on the public
generally.

Foreseeability

     The effect of a decision is "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not
required; however, if an effect is a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198,
copy enclosed.)

     Because any decision to purchase, remodel or construct a
particular building will necessarily eliminate the other buildings
from consideration, it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have an effect on the developer who has been a source of
income to you within the preceding 12 months. Clearly, any deci-
sion will result in either (1) purchase of the developer's build-
ing, in which case the developer will profit from the decision,
or, (2) purchase, refurbishing, or construction of another build-
ing in which case the developer will fail to realize a profit from
the sale of the building to the county. Whether the effect of the
decision on the developer is positive or negative is irrelevant
for purposes of the Act. (Jung Advice Letter, No. A-87-154, copy
enclosed.)

Materiality

     Additionally, we conclude that it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision to either purchase a building, remodel existing
space, or erect a new building, will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the developer who owns one of the buildings under
consideration.

     We first consider situations where the decisions in question
would directly affect the developer who is a source of income to
you. In this case, Regulation 18702.1 (copy enclosed) requires
you to disqualify yourself from participating in a decision if:

     (1) Any person (including a business entity)
which has been source of income (including gifts)
to the official of $250 or more in the preceding 12
months is directly involved in a decision before
the official's agency.... (Emphasis added.)

                          Regulation 18702.1(a)(1).

     A person or business entity is directly involved in a deci-
sion before an official's agency when that person or entity,
either personally or by an agent:

Larry T. Combs
April 24, 1989
Page 6


          (1) Initiates the proceeding in which the
decision will be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request or;

          (2) Is a named party in, or is the subject
of, the proceeding concerning the decision before
the official or the official's agency.

          (3) A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation
of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person or business
entity. (Emphasis added.)

                              Regulation 18702.1(b).

     As discussed above, the developer whose building is under
consideration for purchase by the county has been a source of
income in excess of $250 to you because of your community property
interest in your spouse's income. Therefore, you are disqualified
from participating in any decision which would directly affect the
developer, such as a decision concerning a contract between the
county and the developer for the sale of the developer's building.
(Regulation 18702.1(a)(1).) Similarly, you must abstain from
participating in decisions on occupancy and layout questions for
the developer's building while the county is in the process of
considering purchase of a building.

     Additionally, a public official must disqualify himself from
participating in a decision which will have a indirect effect on
his economic interests. Regulation 18702.2 (copy enclosed)
governs this situation. It provides that the effect of a decision
is material as to a business entity in which an official has an
economic interest if:

          (1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or more; or

          (2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or

          (3) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or more.

                              Regulation 18702.2(g).

Larry T. Combs
April 24, 1989
Page 7


We assume your situation would be covered by subdivision (g).2

     Presumably, the decisions in question will foreseeably affect
the developer's business in the sums set forth above, again
requiring your disqualification. We emphasize, however, that you
are required to disqualify yourself from these decisions only
while the county is considering purchase of the developer's build-
ing as one of its options. If the county were to reject purchase
of the developer's building, you would then be able to participate
in other decisions about the county's space needs.

Public Generally

     Section 87103 provides that an official may participate in a
governmental decision which would foreseeably and materially af-
fect his or her economic interests only if the decision will af-
fect the official's economic interests differently than it will
affect the public generally. Regulation 18703 (copy enclosed)
provides that a material financial effect of a governmental
decision on an official's economic interest is distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally unless the decision will
affect the official's interest in substantially the same manner as
it will affect a significant segment of the public. The decision
to purchase a building will not affect the public generally in
substantially the same manner as it will affect the developer.
Thus, this exception is inapplicable to the instant situation. As
a result, disqualification is required as discussed above.

CONCLUSION

     Pursuant to the above discussion, you should abstain from
participating in any discussions regarding whether it is in the
best interest of the county to purchase a building, remodel exist-
ing space or erect a new building. Ultimately, the decisions
reached would have a foreseeable material effect upon the owner of
the building which has been a source of income to you because to
opt for one alternative would effectively result in the rejection
of other alternatives available to the county. Similarly, and due
to the fact that it is reasonably foreseeable that any decision
will have an effect on the developer who has been a source of
income to you, you should abstain from participating in discus-
sions related to occupancy and layout issues regarding the
developer's building while the county's decision on purchase of an

2     Subdivision (g) applies to business entities not qualified
for listing and not listed on the New York or American stock
Exchanges, or the National Association of Securities National
Market List, and not qualified for public sale pursuant to
California Corporations Code Section 25110. Let us know if you
believe a different subdivision applies to your facts.

Larry T. Combs
April 24, 1989
Page 8


existing building is pending. Additionally, you should abstain
from participating in decisions regarding financing, construction,
acquisition of space, sale of county-owned land and related issues
until such time as all decisions related to the purchase of the
developer's building have been reached.

     If you have any questions regarding the above, do not
hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Kathryn E. Donovan
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                   By:  Bianca M. Breeze
                                        Counsel, Legal Division

KED:BMB:plh

Enclosure

County of Sutter                                ...established 1850

Office of the County Administrator


March 20, 1989


California Fair Political
Practices Commission
Attention: Blanca Breeze
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Dear Ms. Breeze:

In our conversation regarding Sutter County considering the pur-
chase of some buildings, it has become apparent that the FPPC
would consider that I have a financial conflict through my wife's
firm which would prevent me from participating in the decision
to, or not to, purchase the buildings. As you might imagine, the
decisions that pertain to the County's space needs are much more
involved than that decision.  I am therefore seeking a written
opinion regarding what limitations, if any, might be present
regarding my involvement.  I do not perceive any, but I wish to
be certain that I am not missing anything.

For several years, Sutter County has struggled with space con-
straints.  One of our specific, needs is to relocate our Health
Department.  In conjunction with that I have several questions:

1) Can I be involved in the discussion, either at the staff
or Board level, regarding whether it is more cost effective, or
to the County's long term benefit, to buy any building, remodel
existing space, or build a new building?  I realize, of course,
that I could not discuss the purchase of the particular building
that would represent a conflict for me.  I do feel, however, that
I should be able to discuss conceptually, the three alternatives
and any cost analysis prepared regarding them.  The cost analysis
would not be building-specific but rather generalized.  My
opinion is based on the fact that there would be a price/benefit
ratio involved, above which purchasing any building would not be
economical.  It would be my intent to limit my discussion to such
a generic level until, or unless, the building in which I had a
financial conflict was eliminated through a parallel decision
making process, not involving me, currently underway.

2)  If I cannot discuss the purchase of a building as long
as the one regarding which, I have a financial conflict is being


463 SECOND STREET                    PHONE 741-7100
YUBA CITY, CALIF. 95991              AREA CODE 916

California Fair Political Practices Commission
March 22, 1988


considered, may I participate in discussions regarding which of
the other alternatives (building a new building or remodeling ex-
isting County-owned space) would be appropriate assuming the
County chooses not to, or is unsuccessful in its efforts to, buy
a building?

     3)     If the answer to 1) and 2) above are "no", may I par-
ticipate in discussions after a decision has been made to either
buy, or not to buy, the building in regard to which I have a
financial conflict?

With regard to the two other buildings being considered for pur-
chase, I have the same conflict through my wife's firm.  The
space considerations involve such things as which departments
will occupy the buildings, when in the future will the County
build additional space on property currently owned by the County
(purchased many years ago to build a County complex), how should
a new building (constructed or purchased) be financed, etc.
Again, I have several questions:

     4)     Can I participate in occupancy and layout questions
regarding the buildings that would represent a conflict?  I do
not see any conflict at all in my participating in a policy deci-
sion based on a "what if we owned this building" scenario.  The
decision to buy, or not buy, those buildings will be made through
a parallel decision making process not involving me.  The deci-
sions regarding which departments to locate in those buildings
will not effect the purchase decision as there are several viable
options. While the decisions regarding occupancy and layout will
only be used if the buildings are purchased, they should be ready
for immediate implementation upon purchase in order to avoid
costly delays.

     5)     Can I participate in the other decisions regarding
financing, construction of a building on County property, timing
of the future construction or acquisition of space, etc.?  I
would note that these decisions involve many County departments
and several County owned parcels of land.

     6)     Can I participate in the decision to sell County owned
land or buildings to finance the County's space needs?  As I am
sure you are aware, there is a specific procedure for selling
such property, involving the Board's adoption of a "Resolution of
Intent to Sell" and the public opening of offers to purchase.


                                    2

California Fair Political Practices Commission
March 22, 1988


In addition to answering the questions that I have asked, please
provide such additional guidance as you consider appropriate.
I mentioned, the County has been involved in assessment and
planning for its needs for several years.  Due to various cir-
cumstances, I am the person in whom much of the overall perspec-
tive resides since I have coordinated the County's effort from
the beginning.  As you are aware, Sutter County is a small county
and I am sure you understand that there are not a lot of people
to whom such studies and decisions can be delegated.  Compounding
the problem is that three new Board members took office in
January.  Based on these, and other factors, the Board wishes to
have my perspective and recommendations to the extent possible.
I would, therefore, appreciate any guidance you can give me.


[signature]
LARRY T. COMBS
County Administrative Officer

LTC:km


3

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 24, 1989


Larry T. Combs
County Administrator
County of Sutter
463 Second Street
Yuba City, Ca  95991

Re:  Letter No. 89-177

Dear Mr. Combs:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform Act
was received on March 24, 1989 by the Fair Political Practices
Commission.  If you have any questions about your advice request,
you may contact Bianca Breeze an attorney in the Legal Division,
directly at (916) 322-5901.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly.  Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, or
more information is needed, you should expect a response within 21
working days if your request seeks formal written advice.  If more
information is needed, the person assigned to prepare a response
to your request will contact you shortly to advise you as to
information needed.  If your request is for informal assistance,
we will answer it as quickly as we can.  (See Commission
Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 18329).)

You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel

sections.conclusion:
1. 
You may not participate in discussions, either at the 
staff or board level, regarding whether it is more cost effective, 
or in the county's long-term best interest to purchase a building, 
remodel existing space, or erect a new building while the county 
is considering purchase of the developer's building as one of its 
options. 
These decisions would foreseeably and materially affect 
the developer who is a source of income to you. 
2. 
You may not participate in discussions regarding the 
remodeling of existing space or the erection of a new county 
building while the county is considering purchase of the 
developer's building as one of its options. 
3. 
You may not participate in discussions leading to a 
determination of which building to purchase, if any. 
Moreover, 
once the decision to purchase a particular building has been 
reached, you may not participate in discussions affecting the 
purchase of the building if the building selected for purchase is 
the one owned by the developer who is a source of income to you. 
4. 
You may not participate in occupancy and layout questions 
regarding the building that would present a conflict of interest 
while the county is considering purchase of the developer's 
building as one of its options. 
5. 
You may not participate in decisions regarding financing, 
construction of a building on county property, timing of future 
construction, acquisition of space and related issues while the 
county is considering purchase of the developer's building as one 
of its options. 
6. 
You may not participate in the decision to sell county-
owned land or buildings to finance the county's space needs until 
after such time as the county has reached a decision to purchase 
or not to purchase the developer's building. 


Larry T. Combs 
April 24, 1989

sion.

income to you in a manner different from the effect on the public

or, (2) purchase, refurbishing, or construction of another build-
ing in which case the developer will fail to realize a profit from

Whether the effect of the

(Junq Advice Letter, No. A-87-154, copy 
enclosed. )

that the decision to either purchase a building, remodel existing

effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, 
on the developer who owns one of the buildings under 
consideration. 
We first consider situations where the decisions in question 
would_directlY affect the developer who is a source of income to 
you. 
In this case, Regulation 18702.1 (copy enclosed) requires 
you to disqualify yourself from participating in a decision if: 
(1) 
Any person (including a business entity) 
which has been source of income (including gifts) 
to the official of $250 or more in the preceding 12 
months is directly involved in a decision before 
the official's agency.... 
(Emphasis added.) 
Regulation 18702.1(a) (1). 
A person or business entity is directly involved in a deci-
sion before an official's agency when that person or entity, 
either personally or by an agent: 


Larry T. Combs 
April 24, 1989  
(1) 
Initiates the proceeding in which the 
decision will be made by filing an application, 
claim, appeal, or similar request or; 
(2) 
Is a named party in, or is the subject 
of, the proceeding concerning the decision before 
the official or the official's agency. 
(3) 
A person or business entity is the 
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the 
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation 
of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or 
contract with, the subject person or business 
entity. 
(Emphasis added.) 
Regulation 18702.1(b). 
As discussed above, the developer whose building is under 
consideration for purchase by the county has been a source of 
income in excess of $250 to you because of your community property 
interest in your spouse's income. 
Therefore, you are disqualified 
from participating in any decision which would directly affect the 
developer, such as a decision concerning a contract between the 
county and the developer for the sale of the developer's building. 
(Regulation 18702.1(a) (1).) 
Similarly, you must abstain from 
participating in decisions on ~ccupancy and layout questions for 
the developer's building while the county is in the process of 
considering purchase of a building. 
Additionally, a public official must disqualify himself from 
participating in a decision which will have a indirect effect on 
his economic interests. 
Regulation 18702.2 (copy enclosed) 
governs this situation. 
It provides that the effect of a decision 
is material as to a business entity in which an official has an 
economic interest if: 
(1) 
The decision will result in an increase 
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year 
of $10,000 or more; or 
(2) 
The decision will result in the business 
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or 
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a 
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or 
(3) 
The decision will result in an increase 
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities 
of $10,000 or more. 
Regulation 18702.2(g). 


Larry T. Combs 
April 24, 1989  
We assume your situation would be covered by subdivision (g).2 
Presumably, the decisions in question will foreseeably affect 
the developer's business in the sums set forth above, again 
requiring your disqualification. 
We emphasize, however, that you 
are required to disqualify yourself from these decisions only 
while the county is considering purchase of the developer's build-
ing as one of its options. 
If the county were to reject purchase 
of the developer's building, you would then be able to participate 
in other decisions about the county's space needs. 
Public Generally 
section 87103 provides that an official may participate in a 
governmental decision which would foreseeably and materially af-
fect his or her economic interests only if the decision will af-
fect the official's economic interests differently than it will 
affect the public generally. 
Regulation 18703 (copy enclosed) 
provides that a material financial effect of a governmental 
decision on an official's economic interest is distinguishable 
from its effect on the public generally unless the decision will 
affect the official's interest in substantially the same manner as 
it will affect a significant segment of the public. 
The decision 
to purchase a building will not affect the public generally in 
substantially the same manner as it will affect the developer. 
Thus, this exception is inapplicable to the instant situation. 
As 
a result, disqualification is required as discussed above. 
CONCLUSION 
Pursuant to the above discussion, you should abstain from 
participating in any discussions regarding whether it is in the 
best interest of the county to purchase a building, remodel exist-
ing space or erect a new building. 
Ultimately, the decisions 
reached would have a foreseeable material effect upon the owner of 
the building which has been a source of income to you because to 
opt for one alternative would effectively result in the rejection 
of other alternatives available to the county. 
Similarly, and due 
to the fact that it is reasonably foreseeable that any decision 
will have an effect on the developer who has been a source of 
income to you, you should abstain from participating in discus-
sions related to occupancy and layout issues regarding the 
developer's building while the county's decision on purchase of an 
2 
Subdivision (g) applies to business entities not qualified 
for listing and not listed on the New York or American stock 
Exchanges, or the National Association of Securities National 
Market List, and not qualified for public sale pursuant to 
California corporations Code section 25110. 
Let us know if you 
believe a dif£erent subdivision applies to your facts. 


Larry T. Combs 
April 24, 1989  
existing building is pending. 
Additionally, you should abstain 
from participating in decisions regarding financing, construction, 
acquisition of space, sale of county-owned land and related issues 
until such time as all decisions related to the purchase of the 
developer's building have been reached.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. 
Under the Act, may you participate in discussions, either 
at the staff or board level, regarding whether it is more cost 
effective, or in the county's long-term best interest, to purchase 
a building, remodel existing space, or erect a new building? 
The 
county is considering, among other options, purchasing a building 
owned by a developer who is a client of your wife's business. 
2. 
If you may not participate in discussions regarding the 
purchase of a building because of your financial interest in that 
decision, may you participate in decisions related to remodeling 
existing space or the erection of a new building? 
3. 
If you may not participate in the above discussions, may 
you participate in discussions after a decision has been made to 
, et seq. 
All references to regulations are to Title 2, 
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 
 • (916) 322-5660 


Larry T. Combs 
April 24, 1989  
either purchase, or not to purchase, the building with respect to 
which you have a conflict of interest? 
4. 
May you participate in occupancy and layout questions 
regarding the building that would present a conflict of interest? 
5. 
~ay you participate in decisions regarding financing, 
construction of a building on county property, timing of future 
construction, acquisition of space and related issues? 
6. 
May you participate in the decision to sell county-owned 
land or buildings to finance the county's space needs?

CONCLUSION: 1. 
You may not participate in discussions, either at the 
staff or board level, regarding whether it is more cost effective, 
or in the county's long-term best interest to purchase a building, 
remodel existing space, or erect a new building while the county 
is considering purchase of the developer's building as one of its 
options. 
These decisions would foreseeably and materially affect 
the developer who is a source of income to you. 
2. 
You may not participate in discussions regarding the 
remodeling of existing space or the erection of a new county 
building while the county is considering purchase of the 
developer's building as one of its options. 
3. 
You may not participate in discussions leading to a 
determination of which building to purchase, if any. 
Moreover, 
once the decision to purchase a particular building has been 
reached, you may not participate in discussions affecting the 
purchase of the building if the building selected for purchase is 
the one owned by the developer who is a source of income to you. 
4. 
You may not participate in occupancy and layout questions 
regarding the building that would present a conflict of interest 
while the county is considering purchase of the developer's 
building as one of its options. 
5. 
You may not participate in decisions regarding financing, 
construction of a building on county property, timing of future 
construction, acquisition of space and related issues while the 
county is considering purchase of the developer's building as one 
of its options. 
6. 
You may not participate in the decision to sell county-
owned land or buildings to finance the county's space needs until 
after such time as the county has reached a decision to purchase 
or not to purchase the developer's building. 


Larry T. Combs 
April 24, 1989

Combs County Administrative Officer County of Sutter 463 Second Street Yuba City, CA 95991 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-89-177 Dear Mr. Combs: This is in response to your request for advice regarding your responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Your letter raises questions under other provisions of law, such as Government Code Section 1090. We do not provide advice concerning laws other than the Act. Please consult with your county counsel or the Attorney General's office regarding other applicable laws which may have an impact on your decision to purchase a building for the county. QUESTIONS 1. Under the Act, may you participate in discussions, either at the staff or board level, regarding whether it is more cost effective, or in the county's long-term best interest, to purchase a building, remodel existing space, or erect a new building? The county is considering, among other options, purchasing a building owned by a developer who is a client of your wife's business. 2. If you may not participate in discussions regarding the purchase of a building because of your financial interest in that decision, may you participate in decisions related to remodeling existing space or the erection of a new building? 3. If you may not participate in the above discussions, may you participate in discussions after a decision has been made to ___________________________ 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. Combs April 24, 1989 Page 2 either purchase, or not to purchase, the building with respect to which you have a conflict of interest? 4. May you participate in occupancy and layout questions regarding the building that would present a conflict of interest? 5. May you participate in decisions regarding financing, construction of a building on county property, timing of future construction, acquisition of space and related issues? 6. May you participate in the decision to sell county-owned land or buildings to finance the county's space needs? CONCLUSIONS 1. You may not participate in discussions, either at the staff or board level, regarding whether it is more cost effective, or in the county's long-term best interest to purchase a building, remodel existing space, or erect a new building while the county is considering purchase of the developer's building as one of its options. These decisions would foreseeably and materially affect the developer who is a source of income to you. 2.
